title: The Koji Path: A Spirit Built by a Mold
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain what makes sake unique and how this episode studies it.
Subject: Episode introduction | Run time: about 3 minutes

[WELCOME] Welcome back to Better Vice Club, Season 2, The Oldest Toast. This is Anthony McDonald. We have followed grain, grape, cane, and agave. Today we cross to Japan and meet sake, made from rice, water, and a quiet partner most people have never heard of: a mold called koji.

[HOOK] The honest note first. This is a history and culture class, not a bar, and we will be straight about alcohol's harms. Now the hook. Every other drink this season started with a plant that already held sugar, or with grain that sprouts to make its own. Rice does not. Rice is pure starch, locked up tight, and yeast cannot ferment starch. So how do you make wine from rice? The Japanese answer is one of the most elegant pieces of biotechnology in the world: you let a mold do the unlocking. That mold is koji, and following it is the whole path of this episode.

[TEACH] We read sake through four lenses. Geography asks how koji works, how three Japanese towns make three different sakes from three different waters, and how rice-wine traditions spread across all of Asia. Social studies asks how sake became sacred in Shinto, how Japanese colonizers suppressed Korea's rice wine, and why nearly every fermented drink began in a sacred place. Economics asks why sake is shrinking at home but booming abroad, and why the polishing of a grain of rice is the most honest price tag in the season. English language arts asks about words English had to borrow, like umami and wabi-sabi, because it had no way to say them. Each lesson is short and ends with a quick question.

[REMEMBER] Hold this. Sake is unique because rice holds no free sugar, so a mold called koji must first turn the rice starch into sugar before yeast can make alcohol. Sake is less a farmed drink than a partnership with a microorganism, and that partnership is the koji path.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does making sake require koji mold, when beer and wine do not need it? [Beat] Rice is pure starch with no free sugar, and yeast cannot ferment starch, so koji must first convert the starch into sugar.

## Key Takeaways
- Sake is made from rice, water, and koji, a mold that converts rice starch into fermentable sugar.
- Unlike grain, grape, cane, or agave, rice holds no free sugar, so koji is essential.
- This episode is educational and honest about alcohol's harms; it is not an endorsement of drinking.
- Better Vice Club reads sake through geography, social studies, economics, and English language arts.

## Sources
- Gauntner, J. (2002). The sake handbook. Tuttle Publishing.

title: Koji, the National Mold
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain what koji does and why it underpins an entire cuisine.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we opened the sake episode. Two quick questions.
One: why does sake need koji when beer and wine do not? [Beat] Rice is pure starch with no free sugar, and yeast cannot ferment starch (Gauntner, 2002).
Two: what three things is sake made from? [Beat] Rice, water, and koji.

[HOOK] In 2006, Japan did something no other country has done. It named a national mold. Not a national bird or flower, a microorganism. That tells you how central this one tiny organism is to Japanese life, because it does far more than make sake.

[TEACH] The mold is Aspergillus oryzae, called koji, and its job is to convert starch into sugar (Shurtleff & Aoyagi, 2012). Grown on steamed rice, koji releases enzymes that break the locked-up rice starch into sugars that yeast can then ferment into alcohol. And here is what makes sake special as biochemistry. In beer, you first convert grain to sugar, then ferment. In sake, the koji converting starch to sugar and the yeast turning sugar to alcohol happen at the same time, in the same vessel, a process called multiple parallel fermentation (Gauntner, 2002). It is one of the more sophisticated fermentations humans ever worked out, and it was done by trial and patience long before anyone could see a microbe.
Now widen the lens. Koji does not stop at sake. The same mold ferments soybeans and grain into miso, soybeans and wheat into soy sauce, rice into mirin and rice vinegar (Shurtleff & Aoyagi, 2012). One mold sits underneath the savory backbone of an entire cuisine. That is why naming it the National Mold made sense: koji is cultural infrastructure, a living organism that an entire food tradition is built on top of.

[PICTURE] Picture a warm, cedar-lined room where a brewer dusts pale green koji spores over a bed of steaming rice and tends it like a gardener. From that one bed of moldy rice flows not just sake, but the miso in the soup, the soy sauce on the table, and the mirin in the glaze. One organism, a whole kitchen.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: koji is the mold Aspergillus oryzae, which converts rice starch into fermentable sugar, lets sake ferment by multiple parallel fermentation, and also underlies miso, soy sauce, and mirin, which is why Japan named it the National Mold (Shurtleff & Aoyagi, 2012; Gauntner, 2002). One mold holds up a cuisine.

[CHECK] Quick check. What makes sake's fermentation, multiple parallel fermentation, unusual? [Beat] The koji turning starch to sugar and the yeast turning sugar to alcohol happen at the same time in the same vessel (Gauntner, 2002).

## Key Takeaways
- Koji is the mold Aspergillus oryzae, which converts rice starch into fermentable sugar (Shurtleff & Aoyagi, 2012).
- Sake uses multiple parallel fermentation, where koji and yeast work at the same time in one vessel (Gauntner, 2002).
- The same koji also makes miso, soy sauce, mirin, and rice vinegar, the savory backbone of Japanese food.
- Japan named koji its National Mold in 2006, recognizing a microorganism as cultural infrastructure.

## Sources
- Shurtleff, W., & Aoyagi, A. (2012). History of koji, grains and/or soybeans enrobed with a mold culture. Soyinfo Center.
- Gauntner, J. (2002). The sake handbook. Tuttle Publishing.

title: Three Waters, Three Sakes
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how local water shapes sake and how this completes the season's water-chemistry story.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we met koji. Two quick questions.
One: what does koji convert, and into what? [Beat] Rice starch into fermentable sugar (Shurtleff & Aoyagi, 2012).
Two: name one other food koji makes besides sake. [Beat] Miso, soy sauce, mirin, or rice vinegar (Shurtleff & Aoyagi, 2012).

[HOOK] Three Japanese sake towns, two of them only about 50 miles apart, make three completely different styles. The grapes, sorry, the rice and the skill are similar. The difference is the water. And this is where the whole season clicks together.

[TEACH] Sake is about 80 percent water, so the local water writes itself into the sake (Gautier, 2018). Nada, near Kobe, has hard, mineral-rich water full of phosphorus and potassium, which feeds a strong fermentation and gives a bold, structured, assertive sake. Fushimi, in Kyoto, just a short distance away, has soft, gentle, low-mineral water, and it gives a delicate, refined, elegant sake. And Niigata, up in snow country, has very soft, pure snowmelt water, which gives a clean, dry, precise sake.
Now step back, because this is the spine of the entire season. We started Season 2 with beer, where Plzen's soft water made Pilsner and Burton's hard sulfate water made bitter IPA. We saw Dublin's hard water make stout, and Kentucky's iron-free limestone water make bourbon. Now Japan completes the pattern: Nada's hard water, Fushimi's soft water, Niigata's pure snowmelt. Every episode, the same chain has held: geology shapes the water, the water shapes the fermentation, and the fermentation shapes the drink (Gautier, 2018). Sake is the final proof of the rule.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Three pins in Japan: hard-water Nada making a bold sake, soft-water Fushimi making an elegant one, snowmelt Niigata making a dry one. Then stretch the map back across the world to Plzen, Dublin, Burton, and Kentucky. One long chain of towns, each one tasting of the rock beneath it.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: sake is mostly water, so Nada's hard water makes bold sake, Fushimi's soft water makes elegant sake, and Niigata's snowmelt makes dry sake, completing Season 2's chain of geology to water to fermentation to drink (Gautier, 2018). Water is the spine of the season.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why do Nada and Fushimi make such different sake despite being close together? [Beat] Nada has hard, mineral-rich water for a bold sake, while Fushimi has soft, gentle water for an elegant one (Gautier, 2018).

## Key Takeaways
- Sake is about 80 percent water, so local water strongly shapes its style (Gautier, 2018).
- Nada's hard, mineral-rich water makes bold sake; Fushimi's soft water makes elegant sake; Niigata's snowmelt makes dry sake.
- This completes Season 2's water-chemistry chain, from Plzen and Dublin to Burton, Kentucky, and Japan.
- The consistent rule: geology shapes water, water shapes fermentation, fermentation shapes the drink.

## Sources
- Gautier, L. (2018). Sake: The history, stories and craft of Japan's artisanal breweries. University of California Press.
- Shurtleff, W., & Aoyagi, A. (2012). History of koji, grains and/or soybeans enrobed with a mold culture. Soyinfo Center.

title: The Rice-Wine Belt of Asia
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how rice-wine traditions spread across Asia alongside rice farming.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we tasted three waters. Two quick questions.
One: which town's hard water makes a bold sake? [Beat] Nada, near Kobe (Gautier, 2018).
Two: what season-long chain does sake complete? [Beat] Geology to water to fermentation to drink (Gautier, 2018).

[HOOK] Sake is the most famous rice wine, but it is far from the only one, and it is not even the oldest. Across Asia, wherever people grew rice, they learned to ferment it, each tradition shaped by its own water and culture. The drink followed the crop.

[TEACH] Rice fermentation grew up across Asia in parallel, each with its own starter culture (Huang, 2000). In China, huangjiu, or yellow wine, has been documented for more than 4,000 years, made with a mixed-mold starter called qu, which makes it older than sake by at least two millennia. In Korea, makgeolli is a cloudy, tangy, low-alcohol rice wine made with nuruk. In the Philippine highlands, tapuy; in northeastern Thailand, sato; in the Vietnamese highlands, ruou can, drunk together from a shared jar. Japan's own sake, made with koji, is one branch of this very old family tree (Huang, 2000).
Here is the geographic point. If you laid a map of Asia's rice-wine traditions over a map of Asia's rice farming, they would line up almost exactly. Where rice could be grown reliably and in abundance, a fermentation tradition appeared. The beverage followed the crop, just as beer followed barley and wine followed the grape. Culture grows where agriculture allows it.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Pins scattered across East and Southeast Asia, Japan, China, Korea, the Philippines, Thailand, Vietnam, each a different rice wine. Now imagine the green of rice paddies underneath every pin. The drinks sit exactly where the rice grows.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: rice-wine traditions developed across Asia in parallel, from China's 4,000-year-old huangjiu to Korea's makgeolli to Japan's sake, and they map almost exactly onto rice agriculture, because the beverage followed the crop (Huang, 2000). Geography decides where a drink can be born.

[CHECK] Quick check. Which rice wine is older than sake, and by roughly how much? [Beat] Chinese huangjiu, documented for more than 4,000 years, older than sake by at least 2,000 years (Huang, 2000).

## Key Takeaways
- Rice-fermentation traditions grew up across Asia in parallel, each with its own starter culture (Huang, 2000).
- Chinese huangjiu, at more than 4,000 years, is older than sake by at least two millennia.
- Korea's makgeolli, the Philippines' tapuy, Thailand's sato, and Vietnam's ruou can are all part of the family.
- Rice-wine geography maps onto rice-farming geography, because the beverage followed the crop.

## Sources
- Huang, H. T. (2000). Science and civilisation in China: Vol. 6, Biology and biological technology, Part V: Fermentations and food science. Cambridge University Press.
- Gautier, L. (2018). Sake: The history, stories and craft of Japan's artisanal breweries. University of California Press.

title: Sake and the Sacred
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain sake's place in Shinto and what its earliest production reveals about women's role.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we walked the rice-wine belt of Asia. Two quick questions.
One: which rice wine is older than sake, and by roughly how much? [Beat] Chinese huangjiu, documented for more than 4,000 years, older than sake by at least 2,000 years (Huang, 2000).
Two: why does rice-wine geography line up so closely with rice farming? [Beat] Because the beverage followed the crop. Wherever rice grew reliably, a fermentation tradition appeared (Huang, 2000).

[HOOK] In Japan you can walk into a shrine and find barrels of sake stacked at the entrance, offered to the spirits. Sake is not just a drink here. It is a way of speaking to the divine. And its very first form was made in a way that tells us who held the knowledge of fermentation long ago.

[TEACH] Start with Shinto, Japan's indigenous spiritual tradition. Shinto centers on kami, the spirits found in nature, in ancestors, and in particular places (Gautier, 2018). Sake sits at the heart of this practice. When sake is offered to the kami at a shrine, it is called o-miki. At celebrations, brewers and guests perform kagami biraki, the ceremonial opening of a sake barrel, breaking the lid and sharing the sake together. Sake appears at weddings, at new year, and at the seasonal festivals that mark the turning of the year. Some shrines still brew their own (Gautier, 2018). The drink is woven into how communities mark time and stay connected to the divine.
Now go back to the beginning. The earliest sake was called kuchikami-zake, which means mouth-chewed sake. Shrine maidens chewed cooked rice and spit it into a vessel. The enzymes in their saliva started breaking rice starch into sugar, and wild yeast turned that sugar into alcohol (Gautier, 2018). I share this plainly, not for shock. It matters because of who did it. Women, often shrine maidens, were the original keepers of fermentation knowledge. The work was ritual, performed inside specific cultural roles, not industry.
Then the picture changed. As sake production industrialized during the Meiji period, roughly 1868 to 1912, women were increasingly pushed out of brewing (Gautier, 2018). The same thing happened in Europe, where women were central to brewing beer for centuries before they were displaced as the work became industry, a pattern we traced back in Episode 8. Two distant cultures, the same quiet shift: women hold the knowledge first, then industrialization takes the work away from them.

[PICTURE] Picture two scenes side by side. In the first, a shrine maiden a thousand years ago, chewing rice and gathering it in a wooden vessel, the keeper of a sacred craft. In the second, a Meiji-era sake factory, busy and mechanized, with the women gone from the brewing floor. The same drink connects the two scenes, but the hands that make it have changed.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: sake is central to Shinto, offered as o-miki and shared through kagami biraki, and its earliest form, kuchikami-zake, was made by women who were the original keepers of fermentation knowledge until industrialization pushed them out (Gautier, 2018). The sacred and the gendered history sit together in one cup.

[CHECK] Quick check. What does kuchikami-zake reveal about women's historical role in sake? [Beat] Shrine maidens made the earliest sake by chewing rice, which shows women were the original keepers of fermentation knowledge before industrialization excluded them (Gautier, 2018).

## Key Takeaways
- In Shinto, sake is offered to the kami as o-miki and shared through the barrel-opening ceremony kagami biraki (Gautier, 2018).
- Sake marks weddings, new year, and seasonal festivals, and some shrines still brew their own.
- The earliest sake, kuchikami-zake, was made by shrine maidens chewing rice, so women were the original keepers of fermentation knowledge.
- Industrialization in the Meiji period (1868 to 1912) pushed women out of brewing, echoing how women were displaced from European brewing (Episode 8).

## Sources
- Gautier, L. (2018). Sake: The history, stories and craft of Japan's artisanal breweries. University of California Press.
- Huang, H. T. (2000). Science and civilisation in China: Vol. 6, Biology and biological technology, Part V: Fermentations and food science. Cambridge University Press.

title: The Suppression of Makgeolli
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how Japanese colonial rule suppressed Korea's rice wine and what its revival means.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we sat with sake and the sacred. Two quick questions.
One: what is o-miki? [Beat] Sake offered to the kami at a Shinto shrine (Gautier, 2018).
Two: what does kuchikami-zake reveal about women's historical role? [Beat] Shrine maidens made the earliest sake by chewing rice, so women were the original keepers of fermentation knowledge (Gautier, 2018).

[HOOK] Korea had its own rice wine long before this story begins: makgeolli, cloudy and tangy, brewed in homes for centuries. Then a foreign government decided who was allowed to make it. This is a history that rarely gets told in sake conversations, and it belongs here.

[TEACH] During Japan's colonial occupation of Korea, from 1910 to 1945, the control reached past politics and economy into food culture (Park & Kim, 2019). Korean home brewing of makgeolli, a household practice going back centuries, was restricted through Japanese licensing requirements. The licenses were built to centralize production under approved facilities, which pushed makgeolli out of ordinary kitchens. Korean breweries were forced to adopt Japanese sake methods and equipment. And traditional nuruk cultivation was discouraged in favor of Japanese koji (Park & Kim, 2019).
That last point matters more than it might sound. Nuruk is Korea's fermentation starter, and it depends on specific regional knowledge and on microbial communities, the molds, yeasts, and bacteria, that live in a particular place. When the practice is discouraged across a generation, that living knowledge does not just pause. It nearly disappears. Many nuruk traditions were almost lost during the occupation (Park & Kim, 2019).
Here is why this was a deep loss, not just a culinary one. Makgeolli was woven into Korean agricultural life. People drank it at planting and at harvest. They made it in the home as an ordinary household skill (Park & Kim, 2019). To suppress makgeolli was to suppress a form of community and cultural practice, the shared rhythms of a farming year and a domestic craft passed between generations.
Now the hopeful turn. Since the 2000s, Korea has been reviving makgeolli (Park & Kim, 2019). Brewers, food scientists, and elders who retained partial knowledge have worked together to recover the microbial diversity that almost vanished. So the revival is three things at once. It is a craft beverage movement. It is a heritage-recovery project, rebuilding knowledge that was nearly erased. And it is an assertion of Korean identity, distinct from Japanese influence (Park & Kim, 2019). Reviving the starter is also reviving the self.

[PICTURE] Picture a Korean kitchen in the 1930s where the family once brewed makgeolli, now quiet, the brewing moved to a licensed facility far away. Then picture a small workshop today: a young brewer, a food scientist with a microscope, and an elder who remembers, leaning over a tray of nuruk, coaxing back a culture that almost died. The same starter connects loss and recovery.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Japanese colonial rule (1910 to 1945) restricted Korean home brewing of makgeolli through licensing, forced Japanese sake methods on Korean breweries, and discouraged nuruk so that many traditions were nearly lost, while the revival since the 2000s is a craft movement, a heritage recovery, and an assertion of Korean identity (Park & Kim, 2019). Suppressing a drink can suppress a culture.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why is the modern makgeolli revival more than a craft movement? [Beat] Because it recovers microbial knowledge nearly lost under occupation and asserts Korean identity, making it a heritage-recovery project as much as a beverage trend (Park & Kim, 2019).

## Key Takeaways
- During Japan's occupation of Korea (1910 to 1945), home brewing of makgeolli was restricted through Japanese licensing (Park & Kim, 2019).
- Korean breweries were forced to adopt Japanese sake methods, and nuruk cultivation was discouraged in favor of Japanese koji, so many nuruk traditions were nearly lost.
- Makgeolli was woven into Korean agricultural life, drunk at planting and harvest and made in the home, so suppressing it suppressed a cultural practice.
- The revival since the 2000s is at once a craft movement, a heritage-recovery project, and an assertion of Korean identity.

## Sources
- Park, S. J., & Kim, Y. H. (2019). The revival of traditional Korean rice wine makgeolli. Journal of Ethnic Foods, 6(1), 1-9.

title: Why Fermentation Is Sacred
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can reflect on why nearly every fermented drink in the season began in a sacred context.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we sat with the suppression of makgeolli. Two quick questions.
One: name one way Japan suppressed makgeolli during the occupation. [Beat] Restricting home brewing through licensing, forcing Japanese sake methods on Korean breweries, or discouraging nuruk in favor of Japanese koji (Park & Kim, 2019).
Two: why is the revival more than a craft movement? [Beat] Because it recovers nearly lost microbial knowledge and asserts Korean identity, a heritage-recovery project as much as a beverage trend (Park & Kim, 2019).

[HOOK] We are near the end of Season 2, so step back and look at the whole table. Beer, wine, whiskey, tequila, sake. Almost every one of them began in a sacred place. That is not a small coincidence. It is a pattern worth sitting with.

[TEACH] Walk the pattern slowly. Beer appears in the Sumerian Hymn to Ninkasi, addressed to the goddess of beer herself. Wine carries two sacred frames, the Greek god Dionysus and the Christian Eucharist, where wine becomes part of worship (Cwiertka, 2006). Whiskey grew out of the Arab alchemical tradition and the monastic stills of medieval Europe, a craft tied to medicine and the spiritual life. Tequila and mezcal trace back to the Aztec goddess Mayahuel, and before them pulque was a regulated sacred drink. And sake, our subject, lives inside Shinto, offered to the kami as o-miki (Gautier, 2018). One drink stands apart. Rum had no sacred framing. It was a colonial product from the start, made from the sugar of plantation labor.
So here is the reflective question, and I want to hold it as a real question, not a tidy answer. Why did fermented drinks so consistently emerge from sacred or spiritual contexts? Across cultures that had no contact with one another, the same link keeps appearing between a fermented drink and a religious practice (Cwiertka, 2006). That points to something about how human cultures connected altered states to worship, treating the change a drink brings as a doorway toward the divine rather than only an escape from the ordinary. I do not want to claim more than the record shows. The documented pattern is the link itself, repeated again and again.
And notice the second thread running underneath. Again and again, colonial power disrupted these sacred traditions. Aztec pulque ceremonies were suppressed by Spanish colonizers. Korean makgeolli was suppressed under Japanese occupation (Park & Kim, 2019). The sacred drink and the colonial disruption travel together through the season. The beverage changes from episode to episode. The logic of suppression stays the same.

[PICTURE] Picture a single long table set across centuries and continents. A Sumerian temple with beer, a Greek shrine and a church altar with wine, a monastery still with whiskey, an Aztec rite with pulque, a Shinto shrine with sake. Almost every seat is set with a sacred drink. One seat, rum's, is set in a sugar field instead of a temple. The exception makes the pattern easier to see.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: nearly every Season 2 beverage has a documented sacred origin, from Ninkasi and Dionysus to Mayahuel and Shinto's kami, with rum the colonial exception, and again and again colonial power disrupted these sacred traditions (Gautier, 2018; Cwiertka, 2006). The sacred and the fermented are linked across cultures, and so is the harm done to them.

[CHECK] Quick check. Which Season 2 beverage is the exception to the sacred-origin pattern, and why? [Beat] Rum, because it had no sacred framing and was a colonial product from the start (Cwiertka, 2006).

## Key Takeaways
- Nearly every Season 2 drink has a documented sacred origin: beer's Ninkasi, wine's Dionysus and Eucharist, whiskey's alchemical and monastic roots, tequila and mezcal's Mayahuel, and sake's Shinto kami (Gautier, 2018; Cwiertka, 2006).
- Rum is the one exception, a colonial product from the start with no sacred framing.
- The repeated link across unconnected cultures points to how people tied altered states to religious practice, though the record shows the pattern, not the full reason.
- Colonial power disrupted these sacred traditions again and again, from Aztec pulque to Korean makgeolli (Park & Kim, 2019).

## Sources
- Gautier, L. (2018). Sake: The history, stories and craft of Japan's artisanal breweries. University of California Press.
- Cwiertka, K. J. (2006). Modern Japanese cuisine: Food, power and national identity. Reaktion Books.
- Park, S. J., & Kim, Y. H. (2019). The revival of traditional Korean rice wine makgeolli. Journal of Ethnic Foods, 6(1), 1-9.

title: Volume to Value
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why sake is shrinking at home but booming abroad, and what strategy small breweries use to survive.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we asked why fermentation is sacred. Two quick questions.
One: name one Season 2 beverage and its sacred origin. [Beat] Sake and the kami, the Shinto deities, is one good answer.
Two: which Season 2 drink was the exception, with no sacred origin? [Beat] Rum, which came from enslaved workers' discovery on Barbados, a colonial product from the start.

[HOOK] Here is a puzzle. Sake is in deep decline in Japan. People there are drinking far less of it than they used to. And yet sake sells better and better overseas every year. The same drink is dying at home and thriving abroad. How can both be true at once? The answer is one of the cleanest economic stories in the whole season.

[TEACH] Start with the decline at home. Japan's domestic sake drinking peaked back in 1973 at about 1.76 million kiloliters a year, and the country had more than 4,000 breweries (Japan Sake and Shochu Makers Association, 2023). By 2023, domestic drinking had fallen to roughly 480,000 kiloliters, and the number of breweries had dropped to about 1,200 (Japan Sake and Shochu Makers Association, 2023). That is a fall of more than 70 percent in half a century. Younger Japanese drinkers turned to beer, wine, and other spirits, and the old commodity sake market shrank year after year.
Now the other side. While Japanese drinkers walked away, the rest of the world walked toward sake. Exports grew from about 60 million dollars in 2010 to more than 290 million dollars by 2023 (Japan Sake and Shochu Makers Association, 2023). The biggest markets were the United States and China. So exports grew almost fivefold in the same years that home drinking kept sliding.
Put those two facts together and you get a two-tier market. On one tier, cheap commodity sake for the shrinking home market, sold low and made in volume. On the other tier, premium sake for export, sold high to curious drinkers around the world. A small brewery, called a kura, cannot win the volume game anymore. So more and more of them survive by abandoning it. They focus entirely on the premium tier and on export. They make less, but they sell it for more, to people who care.
And if that move sounds familiar, it should. This is the same volume-to-value survival strategy we have already watched twice this season: with artisanal mezcal back in Episode 12, and with American craft beer back in Episode 8. Three different drinks, three different cultures, one identical economic logic. Make less. Charge more. Connect with the people who value the craft.

[PICTURE] Picture two lines on a chart in this lesson's panel. One line, domestic drinking, starts high in 1973 and slides steadily down to 2023. The other line, export value, starts low in 2010 and climbs sharply to 2023. The lines cross like an X. That X is the whole story: as the home market falls away, the world market rises to meet it, and the small kura ride the rising line.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Japan's domestic sake fell more than 70 percent from a 1973 peak of about 1.76 million kiloliters to roughly 480,000 kiloliters by 2023, while exports grew from about 60 million dollars in 2010 to more than 290 million dollars, so small breweries survive by shifting from volume to value, the same craft strategy seen with mezcal and craft beer (Japan Sake and Shochu Makers Association, 2023). Less volume, more value.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does a small kura focus on premium export sake instead of cheap commodity sake? [Beat] Because the home commodity market is shrinking fast, while premium export sells for much more, so making less but charging more is how a small brewery survives, the same volume-to-value strategy seen with mezcal and craft beer (Japan Sake and Shochu Makers Association, 2023).

## Key Takeaways
- Japan's domestic sake drinking fell more than 70 percent, from about 1.76 million kiloliters in 1973 to roughly 480,000 kiloliters by 2023 (Japan Sake and Shochu Makers Association, 2023).
- Over the same span, the number of breweries dropped from more than 4,000 to about 1,200.
- Exports grew nearly fivefold, from about 60 million dollars in 2010 to more than 290 million dollars by 2023, led by the United States and China.
- The result is a two-tier market, and small kura survive by shifting from volume to value, the same strategy seen with mezcal and craft beer.

## Sources
- Japan Sake and Shochu Makers Association. (2023). Annual report on sake production and exports. JSSA Statistical Report.

title: The Polishing Ratio
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how sake's polishing ratio creates the most transparent price signal in the season.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we went from volume to value. Two quick questions.
One: roughly how much has sake's domestic drinking in Japan fallen since 1973? [Beat] More than 70 percent, from about 1.76 million kiloliters to roughly 480,000 kiloliters by 2023 (Japan Sake and Shochu Makers Association, 2023).
Two: what is the two-tier market? [Beat] Cheap commodity sake for the shrinking home market on one tier, premium sake for export on the other.

[HOOK] Most of the time, when a drink costs more, you have to take the maker's word for why. The label says "premium," and you trust it, or you do not. Sake is different. Sake puts a single number on the label that tells you, in plain math, why one bottle costs more than another. That number is the most honest price signal in the entire season.

[TEACH] The number is called seimaibuai, the rice polishing ratio. It is the percentage of each rice grain that is left after milling away the outer layers to reach the starchy core at the center. The outer layers of the grain hold proteins and fats that can muddy the flavor. The clean, starchy core makes the most delicate sake. So brewers grind, or polish, the grain down toward that core, and the polishing ratio tells you how far they went.
That ratio defines the whole grade ladder. Basic table sake leaves 70 percent or more of the grain. Ginjo grade leaves 60 percent or less. Daiginjo grade, the top, leaves 50 percent or less (Gauntner, 2002). There is one more word to know: junmai, which means pure rice, with no added alcohol. So a junmai daiginjo is pure rice sake polished down to half the grain or less.
Now here is the economic math, and it is simple and real. If you polish a grain down to 50 percent, you have ground away half its mass and thrown that half away. So to make the same amount of daiginjo as basic sake, you need roughly twice the rice (Gauntner, 2002). Twice the rice for the same bottle. That is not a story or a feeling. That is a genuine, measurable cost, sitting right there in the ratio.
And that is the key contrast. The polishing ratio is required on the label by law. So unlike the celebrity-tequila premium we looked at in Episode 12, where you pay extra for a famous name, or the sourced-whiskey branding from Episode 10, where the marketing hides where the liquid actually came from, sake's higher price is grounded in something you can verify. A daiginjo really did use more rice. The label proves it. That makes the polishing ratio the most transparent price signal we have seen all season.

[PICTURE] Picture two grains of rice side by side in this lesson's panel. The first is barely touched, still 70 percent of its size, the table sake grain. The second has been polished down to a small white pearl at its center, 50 percent gone, the daiginjo grain. Look at the pile of ground-away rice next to the second grain. That discarded pile is the price difference, made visible. You are not paying for a story. You are paying for that pile.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: seimaibuai is the percentage of the rice grain left after polishing, and it sets the grades, table sake at 70 percent or more, ginjo at 60 percent or less, daiginjo at 50 percent or less, so a daiginjo needs roughly twice the rice, a real cost that is legally on the label, making it the most transparent price signal in the season (Gauntner, 2002). The number on the label is the reason for the price.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why is sake's quality price more honest than a celebrity-tequila or sourced-whiskey premium? [Beat] Because the polishing ratio is legally required on the label and reflects a real, measurable cost, a daiginjo uses roughly twice the rice, while the tequila and whiskey premiums rest on a name or marketing you cannot verify (Gauntner, 2002).

## Key Takeaways
- Seimaibuai is the rice polishing ratio, the percentage of each grain left after milling toward the starchy core (Gauntner, 2002).
- It defines the grades: table sake at 70 percent or more, ginjo at 60 percent or less, daiginjo at 50 percent or less; junmai means no added alcohol.
- Polishing to 50 percent throws away half the grain, so a daiginjo needs roughly twice the rice for the same volume, a genuine cost.
- Because the ratio is legally on the label, sake's quality price is verifiable, the most transparent price signal in the season, unlike celebrity tequila or sourced whiskey.

## Sources
- Gauntner, J. (2002). The sake handbook. Tuttle Publishing.

title: The Toji Guild
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why master sake brewers held real economic power that field laborers did not.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we measured the polishing ratio. Two quick questions.
One: what does seimaibuai measure? [Beat] The percentage of the rice grain left after polishing toward the starchy core (Gauntner, 2002).
Two: why does a daiginjo cost more to make? [Beat] Because polishing to 50 percent throws away half the grain, so it needs roughly twice the rice for the same volume (Gauntner, 2002).

[HOOK] All season we have met the people who do the hard work of making a drink, and almost none of them held much power. The sugarcane cutters, the agave harvesters, the coffee pickers, all worked for low wages and could be replaced. Sake has a different kind of worker. The master brewer of a sake house could name his price and move between breweries like a star free agent. Why did this one craftsman hold real bargaining power when so many others did not?

[TEACH] The master sake brewer is called the toji. Becoming one was not quick. An aspiring toji apprenticed for 5 to 15 years or more before reaching master status (Gautier, 2018). The toji worked inside a guild, a regional professional community that maintained quality standards and protected the reputation of its members. And here is the part that gave the toji power: a toji moved seasonally between breweries, going where the work was, and negotiated his services as a highly skilled contractor. His personal reputation set a brewery's position in the market. A famous toji could lift a kura's standing; a brewery that lost him could lose its name.
That is real bargaining power, and it stands apart from the field labor we have studied. Think of the jimadores who cut agave, the sugarcane cutters, the coffee pickers. Their work was hard, but it was also easy to replace, easy to push toward machines, and not closely tied to the price of the final bottle. The toji's situation was the opposite. His expertise was hard to replicate, hard to automate, and directly tied to product value. When the thing you know is rare and your hands set the quality buyers pay for, you can negotiate. That is why a toji commanded professional wages and the field laborer did not.
But there is a modern challenge, and it is a hard one. Japan's population is aging, and the domestic sake industry is shrinking, the same decline we traced earlier. Together these have created a toji shortage. The average toji is now over 60 years old (Gautier, 2018). Younger people went into more stable industries during Japan's growth decades, so fewer trained to take the masters' place. That pressure is slowly opening a door that was long shut. Some breweries now employ non-traditional toji, including women, who were excluded from brewing since the Meiji industrialization, and foreigners. Necessity is doing what tradition resisted: widening who gets to hold the title.

[PICTURE] Picture a single figure in this lesson's panel, the toji, standing in the steam of the kura with the season's tools around him. Now picture a line of brewery owners outside the door, each hoping to hire him for the winter. He chooses. That picture is the power: one rare, trusted craftsman, and a market of breweries competing for his name. Then picture the same door today, half-open, with a woman and a foreigner stepping through, because there are not enough of the old masters left.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the toji is the master sake brewer who apprenticed for 5 to 15 years or more, worked within a guild, and moved between breweries as a skilled contractor whose reputation set a kura's market position, giving him real bargaining power that field laborers lacked, while today an aging shortage, the average toji is over 60, is opening the role to women and foreigners (Gautier, 2018). Rare skill is real power.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why did a toji hold bargaining power that a coffee picker or sugarcane cutter did not? [Beat] Because the toji's expertise was hard to replicate, hard to automate, and directly tied to product value, so breweries competed to hire him, while field labor was easy to replace and not closely tied to the final price (Gautier, 2018).

## Key Takeaways
- The toji is the master sake brewer, who apprenticed for 5 to 15 years or more before reaching master status (Gautier, 2018).
- Guilds maintained quality standards, and toji moved seasonally between breweries as skilled contractors whose reputation set a brewery's market position.
- This gave toji real bargaining power, unlike jimadores, sugarcane cutters, or coffee pickers, because their skill was hard to replicate, hard to automate, and tied to product value.
- An aging population and shrinking industry have caused a toji shortage, the average is over 60, pushing breweries to employ women and foreigners.

## Sources
- Gautier, L. (2018). Sake: The history, stories and craft of Japan's artisanal breweries. University of California Press.

title: Words English Had to Borrow
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how borrowed words reveal that language shapes what a culture can perceive.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we met the toji guild. Two quick questions.
One: why did toji hold real economic power, unlike field laborers? [Beat] Their expertise was rare, hard to replicate, and directly tied to the kura's product quality, so they had genuine market bargaining power (Gauntner, 2002).
Two: roughly how long did a toji apprentice before becoming a master? [Beat] Often 5 to 15 years or more (Gauntner, 2002).

[HOOK] Here is a strange thing to notice. All season, we have been reaching for words from other languages and pulling them whole into English. Terroir. Umami. Koji. Sake. We did not translate them. We borrowed them. And every time English has to borrow a word, it is quietly confessing something: there was a concept here it had met but never named.

[TEACH] Some ideas cannot be translated, only borrowed. When a language has no word for a thing, the gap is not just linguistic. It points to a cultural gap, a piece of knowledge one tradition worked out and another simply had not (Richie, 2001). Look at the words Season 2 borrowed. Terroir came from French, because English had no single word for the complete taste of a place in what it grows. Wabi-sabi, mono no aware, umami, and koji came from Japanese, each one marking a concept English met, recognized, and had to import because it had nothing of its own to name it with.
The clearest case is umami. In 1908 the Japanese chemist Kikunae Ikeda studied dashi, the savory stock at the base of Japanese cooking, and identified glutamate as the source of its deep, brothy taste. He gave that taste a name: umami (Richie, 2001). Japanese food science accepted it. Western food science did not. The Western model of taste recognized only four basics, sweet, sour, salty, and bitter, so there was no slot for a fifth. Then in 2002, scientists confirmed the actual biological receptors on the tongue that respond to glutamate, and only then did Western science formally accept umami as a fifth basic taste (Richie, 2001). Sit with the timeline. For nearly a century, the taste was right there on Western tongues, in every roasted meat and aged cheese and ripe tomato. Western science could not see it as its own thing, not because it was missing, but because there was no word to hang it on.

[PICTURE] Picture a bowl of clear Japanese stock on a lab bench in 1908. A chemist tastes the savory depth, names it umami, and writes it down. Now slide the same bowl across the world to a Western lab in the same year. The depth is there too. But with no word for it, the Western taster files it under "rich" or "meaty" and moves on, and the thing stays nameless for another ninety-four years until a microscope finds the receptor that the language could not.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: some concepts must be borrowed whole, like terroir, wabi-sabi, mono no aware, umami, and koji, because the receiving language has no equivalent, and umami shows why this matters. Western science could not recognize umami as a distinct taste from 1908 until the receptors were confirmed in 2002, partly because it had no word for it (Richie, 2001). The vocabulary you have shapes what you can consciously notice and talk about.

[CHECK] Quick check. What does the umami story show about the link between language and perception? [Beat] That the available vocabulary shapes what a culture can consciously perceive and discuss, since Western science could not recognize umami as a distinct taste for nearly a century, largely for lack of a word (Richie, 2001).

## Key Takeaways
- Some concepts must be borrowed whole because the receiving language has no equivalent, and a linguistic gap reveals a cultural gap (Richie, 2001).
- Season 2 borrowed terroir from French and wabi-sabi, mono no aware, umami, and koji from Japanese, each marking a concept English met but had not named.
- Kikunae Ikeda named umami in 1908; Western science accepted it as a fifth basic taste only after glutamate receptors were confirmed in 2002 (Richie, 2001).
- The vocabulary a culture has available shapes what it can consciously recognize and discuss.

## Sources
- Richie, D. (2001). A tractate on Japanese aesthetics. Stone Bridge Press.

title: The Beauty of Passing
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how Japanese aesthetics shape how sake is valued, and how poetry carries that idea.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 8 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we talked about words English had to borrow. Two quick questions.
One: what does the umami story show about language and perception? [Beat] The vocabulary a culture has shapes what it can perceive, since Western science could not recognize umami as a distinct taste for nearly a century, largely for lack of a word (Richie, 2001).
Two: name one word English borrowed from Japanese. [Beat] Umami, koji, wabi-sabi, or mono no aware (Richie, 2001).

[HOOK] Two of the words we just borrowed are not about taste at all. They are about how to feel about time. Wabi-sabi and mono no aware. And once you understand them, you understand something surprising: they do not just describe a mood. They set the price of a bottle of sake.

[TEACH] Start with wabi-sabi, the beauty found in imperfection and impermanence (Richie, 2001). Then mono no aware, often translated as the pathos of things, a bittersweet awareness that all things pass, and that their passing is itself part of their beauty. The classic symbol is the cherry blossom, which blooms for roughly one week and is treasured precisely because it does not last (Richie, 2001).
Now watch what this does to economics. Premium sake is at its best fresh, often within months of bottling. Its peak is brief, and that brevity is not a flaw to hide; it is part of what makes it precious. Producers and drinkers treat this freshness imperative as a virtue (Richie, 2001). Hold that against whiskey from Episode 10, where the value comes from years in the barrel and age is the selling point. Two cultures, one question: what makes something valuable? Whiskey answers with the premium of aging. Sake answers with the premium of freshness. The same question, opposite answers, and each answer grows straight out of an aesthetic philosophy.
The idea is old, and poetry carries it. Otomo no Tabito, who lived from 665 to 731 CE, was a Japanese nobleman who wrote a famous set of 13 tanka poems in praise of sake, collected in the Man'yoshu, Japan's oldest anthology of poetry, compiled around 759 CE (Keene, 1999). In scholarly paraphrase, Tabito argues that an honest emotional experience, even weeping, is wiser than trying to reason about impermanence with a cold, detached mind. This is not a cheer for getting drunk. It is a claim that authentic feeling is worth more than tidy intellectual distance (Keene, 1999). Set that beside the Persian wine poetry of Episode 9, where wine pointed toward divine love and the soul rising past the world. Same substance in the cup, very different cultural frameworks: Japanese sake poetry leans into accepting that things pass, Persian wine poetry leans toward transcending the world entirely.

[PICTURE] Look at the panel for this lesson: a page from the Man'yoshu. Picture cherry blossoms already starting to fall and a cup of sake set beneath them. Otomo no Tabito watches the petals drop, knows the bloom will be gone within the week, and does not look away. The poem does not mourn that the moment ends. It says the ending is the beauty. Fresh sake, brief blossom, honest tears, all of it precious because none of it lasts.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: wabi-sabi and mono no aware find beauty in imperfection and impermanence, which makes sake's brief freshness part of its value, the economic opposite of whiskey's aging premium, and Otomo no Tabito carried the same idea in his 13 sake poems in the Man'yoshu around 759 CE, prizing honest emotion over detached reasoning (Keene, 1999; Richie, 2001). Beauty can live in the fact that something passes.

[CHECK] Quick check. How is sake's freshness imperative the economic opposite of whiskey's aging premium? [Beat] Sake is valued for its brief peak, best fresh within months, so impermanence adds worth, while whiskey is valued for the years it spends aging, so each culture answers "what makes something valuable" in the opposite way (Richie, 2001).

## Key Takeaways
- Wabi-sabi finds beauty in imperfection and impermanence; mono no aware is the bittersweet awareness that all things pass, symbolized by the one-week cherry blossom (Richie, 2001).
- Premium sake is best fresh within months, so its brief peak is part of its value, the economic opposite of whiskey's aging premium from Episode 10 (Richie, 2001).
- Otomo no Tabito (665 to 731 CE) wrote 13 tanka poems praising sake, collected in the Man'yoshu around 759 CE (Keene, 1999).
- Tabito argues that authentic emotion is wiser than detached intellectualizing, a contrast with the divine-love focus of Persian wine poetry from Episode 9 (Keene, 1999).

## Sources
- Keene, D. (1999). Seeds in the heart: Japanese literature from earliest times to the late sixteenth century. Columbia University Press.
- Richie, D. (2001). A tractate on Japanese aesthetics. Stone Bridge Press.

title: Key Terms: Sake
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can define the core vocabulary from Episode 13 across all four lenses.
Subject: Vocabulary | Run time: about 5 minutes

Your word bank for the Sake episode. Read it quick, out loud if you can. The full glossary lives in the course Resources.

## Geography

**Koji** (KOH-jee). The mold Aspergillus oryzae, which converts rice starch into fermentable sugar; Japan's National Mold (Shurtleff & Aoyagi, 2012).

**Multiple parallel fermentation.** Sake's process where koji turns starch to sugar and yeast turns sugar to alcohol at the same time, in one vessel (Gauntner, 2002).

**Huangjiu** (hwahng-JYOH). Chinese yellow rice wine, documented for over 4,000 years, older than sake (Huang, 2000).

**Makgeolli** (mahk-GUH-lee). A cloudy, tangy Korean rice wine made with nuruk, suppressed under Japanese occupation and now revived (Park & Kim, 2019).

## Social Studies

**Shinto** (SHIN-toh). Japan's indigenous spiritual tradition, centered on kami, in which sake is sacred (Gautier, 2018).

**Kami** (KAH-mee). Shinto spirits residing in nature, ancestors, and places, to whom sake is offered.

**O-miki** (oh-MEE-kee). Sacred sake offered to the kami at Shinto shrines.

**Kuchikami-zake** (koo-chee-KAH-mee ZAH-keh). The earliest sake, made by shrine maidens chewing rice so saliva enzymes began fermentation.

## Economics

**Seimaibuai** (say-my-BOO-eye). The rice polishing ratio, the percentage of each grain left after milling, shown on the label (Gauntner, 2002).

**Junmai** (JOON-my). Pure rice sake, made with no added alcohol.

**Daiginjo** (die-GHIN-joh). A premium sake polished to 50 percent or less of the grain, a high but verifiable cost.

**Toji** (TOH-jee). A master sake brewer and guild member, whose rare expertise gave real bargaining power (Gautier, 2018).

## English Language Arts

**Umami** (oo-MAH-mee). The savory fifth basic taste, named in Japanese in 1908 but recognized in the West only in 2002 (Richie, 2001).

**Wabi-sabi** (WAH-bee SAH-bee). Beauty found specifically in imperfection and impermanence (Richie, 2001).

**Mono no aware** (MOH-noh noh ah-WAH-reh). A bittersweet awareness that all things pass, and that their transience is beautiful.

**Manyoshu** (MAHN-yoh-shoo). Japan's oldest poetry anthology, from about 759 CE, which preserves Otomo no Tabito's sake poems (Keene, 1999).

## Sources
- Gauntner, J. (2002). The sake handbook. Tuttle Publishing.
- Gautier, L. (2018). Sake: The history, stories and craft of Japan's artisanal breweries. University of California Press.
- Huang, H. T. (2000). Science and civilisation in China: Vol. 6, Part V. Cambridge University Press.
- Keene, D. (1999). Seeds in the heart: Japanese literature from earliest times to the late sixteenth century. Columbia University Press.
- Park, S. J., & Kim, Y. H. (2019). The revival of traditional Korean rice wine makgeolli. Journal of Ethnic Foods, 6(1), 1-9.
- Richie, D. (2001). A tractate on Japanese aesthetics. Stone Bridge Press.
- Shurtleff, W., & Aoyagi, A. (2012). History of koji, grains and/or soybeans enrobed with a mold culture. Soyinfo Center.

title: Cumulative Review: Sake
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can recall the top facts from all four lenses and see how they connect.
Subject: Review | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Let's pull the Sake episode together, one quick fact per lens, then the thread that ties them.

[TEACH] Start with geography. Sake is unique because rice holds no free sugar, so a mold called koji must first turn starch into sugar, and koji is so central it became Japan's National Mold, underlying miso, soy sauce, and mirin too (Shurtleff & Aoyagi, 2012). Sake is mostly water, so Nada's hard water makes bold sake and Fushimi's soft water makes elegant sake, completing the season's chain of geology to water to drink (Gautier, 2018). And rice wine grew up across Asia wherever rice grew, from China's 4,000-year-old huangjiu onward (Huang, 2000).

Now social studies. Sake is sacred in Shinto, offered to the kami as o-miki, and its earliest form, kuchikami-zake, shows women were the first keepers of fermentation (Gautier, 2018). Japanese colonizers suppressed Korea's makgeolli during the occupation, and its revival is now an act of cultural identity (Park & Kim, 2019). And nearly every drink this season began in a sacred place, with rum the one exception (Cwiertka, 2006).

Then economics. Sake is shrinking at home but booming in export, so small breweries survive by moving from volume to value (Japan Sake and Shochu Makers Association, 2023). The polishing ratio printed on every label makes sake's quality price the most transparent in the season, grounded in real cost (Gauntner, 2002). And the toji, the master brewer, held real bargaining power that field laborers never had (Gautier, 2018).

Last, language. English had to borrow words like umami and wabi-sabi because it had no way to say them, and for nearly a century Western science could not even perceive umami without the word (Richie, 2001). Japanese aesthetics prize the beauty of passing, so sake is valued fresh, the opposite of whiskey's aging premium, an idea carried in Otomo no Tabito's ancient sake poems (Keene, 1999).

[REMEMBER] Here is the thread. Sake is a partnership with a microorganism, and following koji leads through water and rice farming, through the sacred and the suppressed, through the most honest price tag in the season, and into words and ideas English had to borrow. The koji path runs through everything.

[CHECK] One last check before the quiz. Why is sake unique among the season's drinks? [Beat] Rice holds no free sugar, so the koji mold must first turn its starch into sugar before yeast can make alcohol, a partnership with a microorganism (Gauntner, 2002).

## Key Takeaways
- Geography: koji unlocks rice starch, local water shapes the style, and rice wine spread with rice farming across Asia (Shurtleff & Aoyagi, 2012; Huang, 2000).
- Social studies: sake is sacred in Shinto, women were its first brewers, and Korea's makgeolli was suppressed and revived (Gautier, 2018; Park & Kim, 2019).
- Economics: sake moved from volume to value, the polishing ratio gives transparent pricing, and toji held real bargaining power (Japan Sake and Shochu Makers Association, 2023; Gauntner, 2002).
- Language: English borrowed umami and wabi-sabi, and sake's value lies in its fresh, passing beauty (Richie, 2001; Keene, 1999).

## Sources
- Cwiertka, K. J. (2006). Modern Japanese cuisine. Reaktion Books.
- Gauntner, J. (2002). The sake handbook. Tuttle Publishing.
- Gautier, L. (2018). Sake: The history, stories and craft of Japan's artisanal breweries. University of California Press.
- Huang, H. T. (2000). Science and civilisation in China: Vol. 6, Part V. Cambridge University Press.
- Japan Sake and Shochu Makers Association. (2023). Annual report on sake production and exports. JSSA Statistical Report.
- Keene, D. (1999). Seeds in the heart. Columbia University Press.
- Park, S. J., & Kim, Y. H. (2019). The revival of traditional Korean rice wine makgeolli. Journal of Ethnic Foods, 6(1), 1-9.
- Richie, D. (2001). A tractate on Japanese aesthetics. Stone Bridge Press.
- Shurtleff, W., & Aoyagi, A. (2012). History of koji. Soyinfo Center.
