title: Knowledge Check: Sake
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 13: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

When you finish, take the Project assignment for this episode, where the bigger questions, how one mold holds up a cuisine and how language shapes what we can taste, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"Why does making sake require koji mold when beer and wine do not?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"Rice is pure starch with no free sugar, and yeast cannot ferment starch"},{"id":"q1b","text":"Rice has too much sugar"},{"id":"q1c","text":"Koji adds the alcohol directly"},{"id":"q1d","text":"Rice cannot be fermented at all"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"Rice holds no free sugar, only locked-up starch, and yeast cannot ferment starch, so koji must first convert the starch into sugar.","citation":"Lesson: The Koji Path (intro)"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"What does koji (Aspergillus oryzae) do in sake brewing?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"It converts rice starch into fermentable sugar"},{"id":"q2b","text":"It adds color"},{"id":"q2c","text":"It removes alcohol"},{"id":"q2d","text":"It polishes the rice"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Grown on steamed rice, koji releases enzymes that break starch into sugars that yeast can then ferment into alcohol.","citation":"Lesson: Koji, the National Mold"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"What makes sake's fermentation, called multiple parallel fermentation, unusual?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"The koji turning starch to sugar and the yeast turning sugar to alcohol happen at the same time in one vessel"},{"id":"q3b","text":"It happens in two separate years"},{"id":"q3c","text":"It uses no yeast"},{"id":"q3d","text":"It freezes the rice"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"In beer you first convert grain to sugar, then ferment; in sake both steps happen simultaneously in the same vessel, a sophisticated fermentation.","citation":"Lesson: Koji, the National Mold"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"Why did Japan name koji its National Mold in 2006?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"The same mold underlies sake, miso, soy sauce, and mirin, so it is cultural infrastructure"},{"id":"q4b","text":"It is the only mold in Japan"},{"id":"q4c","text":"It is poisonous"},{"id":"q4d","text":"It is used only for sake"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Koji ferments not just sake but miso, soy sauce, mirin, and rice vinegar, sitting under the savory backbone of an entire cuisine.","citation":"Lesson: Koji, the National Mold"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"Why do Nada and Fushimi make such different sake despite being close together?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"Nada has hard, mineral-rich water for a bold sake; Fushimi has soft water for an elegant one"},{"id":"q5b","text":"They use different rice species only"},{"id":"q5c","text":"One is not real sake"},{"id":"q5d","text":"The climate is opposite"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"Sake is about 80 percent water, so Nada's hard, mineral-rich water makes bold sake while Fushimi's soft water makes a delicate, elegant one.","citation":"Lesson: Three Waters, Three Sakes"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"What season-long chain does sake complete?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"Geology shapes water, water shapes fermentation, fermentation shapes the drink"},{"id":"q6b","text":"Sugar causes all flavor"},{"id":"q6c","text":"Climate alone decides everything"},{"id":"q6d","text":"There is no pattern"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"From Plzen and Dublin to Burton, Kentucky, and Japan's Nada, Fushimi, and Niigata, the same chain holds: geology to water to fermentation to drink.","citation":"Lesson: Three Waters, Three Sakes"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"Which rice wine is older than sake, and by roughly how much?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"Chinese huangjiu, documented for over 4,000 years, older by at least 2,000 years"},{"id":"q7b","text":"Korean makgeolli, by 10,000 years"},{"id":"q7c","text":"None; sake is the oldest"},{"id":"q7d","text":"Thai sato, by 50 years"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"Chinese huangjiu, made with a qu starter, has been documented for more than 4,000 years, making it older than sake by at least two millennia.","citation":"Lesson: The Rice-Wine Belt of Asia"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"Why does rice-wine geography map so closely onto rice-farming geography?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"The beverage followed the crop: where rice grew reliably, a fermentation tradition appeared"},{"id":"q8b","text":"Rice wine causes rice to grow"},{"id":"q8c","text":"It is a coincidence"},{"id":"q8d","text":"Rice wine is made without rice"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"Just as beer followed barley and wine followed the grape, rice wine appeared wherever rice could be grown abundantly, so culture grew where agriculture allowed.","citation":"Lesson: The Rice-Wine Belt of Asia"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"What place does sake hold in Shinto?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"It is offered to the kami as o-miki and used in shrine ceremonies like kagami biraki"},{"id":"q9b","text":"It is forbidden in shrines"},{"id":"q9c","text":"It has no religious role"},{"id":"q9d","text":"It replaced prayer"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"Sake is central to Shinto: offered to the kami as o-miki, opened ceremonially at kagami biraki, and present at weddings, new year, and seasonal festivals.","citation":"Lesson: Sake and the Sacred"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"What does kuchikami-zake reveal about the history of sake?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"The earliest sake was mouth-chewed by shrine maidens, showing women were the original keepers of fermentation"},{"id":"q10b","text":"Sake was always made by machines"},{"id":"q10c","text":"Only emperors made sake"},{"id":"q10d","text":"Sake came from Europe"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"Kuchikami-zake, made by shrine maidens chewing rice so saliva enzymes started fermentation, shows women held the original fermentation knowledge before Meiji industrialization excluded them.","citation":"Lesson: Sake and the Sacred"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"How did Japanese colonial rule suppress Korean makgeolli?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"Through licensing restrictions and forced adoption of Japanese koji methods, nearly destroying traditional nuruk"},{"id":"q11b","text":"By promoting it heavily"},{"id":"q11c","text":"It had no effect on makgeolli"},{"id":"q11d","text":"By exporting it worldwide"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"During the 1910 to 1945 occupation, Korean home brewing was restricted by licensing, breweries were forced to use Japanese methods, and traditional nuruk cultivation was nearly lost.","citation":"Lesson: The Suppression of Makgeolli"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"Why is the modern makgeolli revival more than a craft beverage movement?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"It is also a heritage-recovery project and an assertion of Korean identity distinct from Japanese influence"},{"id":"q12b","text":"It is only about making money"},{"id":"q12c","text":"It has no cultural meaning"},{"id":"q12d","text":"It promotes Japanese sake"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"Recovering nearly lost nuruk microbial diversity is at once a craft movement, a cultural heritage recovery, and an assertion of Korean national identity.","citation":"Lesson: The Suppression of Makgeolli"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"What pattern do nearly all Season 2 beverages share?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"A documented sacred origin, like beer's Ninkasi, wine's Dionysus, and sake's kami"},{"id":"q13b","text":"They were all invented in 1900"},{"id":"q13c","text":"They all come from grapes"},{"id":"q13d","text":"None have any history"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"Beer, wine, whiskey, tequila, mezcal, and sake all have documented sacred origins, with rum the exception, a colonial product from the start.","citation":"Lesson: Why Fermentation Is Sacred"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"Which Season 2 beverage is the exception to the sacred-origin pattern?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"Rum, which was a colonial product from the start"},{"id":"q14b","text":"Sake"},{"id":"q14c","text":"Wine"},{"id":"q14d","text":"Beer"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"Unlike the others, rum had no sacred framing; it emerged as a commodity of the colonial plantation system.","citation":"Lesson: Why Fermentation Is Sacred"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"How has sake's domestic consumption changed since 1973?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"It has fallen more than 70 percent, from about 1.76 million kiloliters to about 480,000 by 2023"},{"id":"q15b","text":"It has doubled"},{"id":"q15c","text":"It has stayed flat"},{"id":"q15d","text":"It vanished entirely"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Japanese domestic sake consumption fell from a 1973 peak of about 1.76 million kiloliters to roughly 480,000 by 2023, a drop of more than 70 percent.","citation":"Lesson: Volume to Value"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"What strategy lets small sake breweries survive the domestic decline?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"Focusing on premium and export, moving from volume to value"},{"id":"q16b","text":"Making only the cheapest sake"},{"id":"q16c","text":"Closing entirely"},{"id":"q16d","text":"Switching to beer"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"Small kura increasingly focus on premium and export, the same volume-to-value survival strategy used by artisanal mezcal and craft beer.","citation":"Lesson: Volume to Value"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"What is seimaibuai?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"The rice polishing ratio, the percentage of each grain left after milling toward the starchy core"},{"id":"q17b","text":"The alcohol percentage"},{"id":"q17c","text":"The price of sake"},{"id":"q17d","text":"A type of yeast"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Seimaibuai is the rice polishing ratio: a daiginjo at 50 percent or less has had at least half of each grain milled away to reach the starchy core.","citation":"Lesson: The Polishing Ratio"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"Why is sake's quality pricing the most transparent in the season?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"The polishing ratio is legally required on the label, reflecting a verifiable production cost, not just a story"},{"id":"q18b","text":"Sake has no quality grades"},{"id":"q18c","text":"Sake prices are random"},{"id":"q18d","text":"It is based on celebrity branding"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"Polishing to 50 percent throws away half the rice, a real measurable cost shown on the label, unlike the narrative premiums of celebrity tequila or sourced whiskey.","citation":"Lesson: The Polishing Ratio"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"Why did toji (master sake brewers) hold real economic power?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"Their hard-to-replicate expertise directly set a brewery's market position, unlike easily replaced field labor"},{"id":"q19b","text":"They owned all the breweries"},{"id":"q19c","text":"They worked for free"},{"id":"q19d","text":"They had no special skills"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"After 5 to 15 years of apprenticeship, a toji's reputation determined a brewery's standing, giving real bargaining power that jimadores and coffee pickers lacked.","citation":"Lesson: The Toji Guild"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"What is the modern challenge facing the toji tradition?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"An aging shortage, pushing breweries to employ women and foreigners as new toji"},{"id":"q20b","text":"Too many young toji"},{"id":"q20c","text":"A ban on brewing"},{"id":"q20d","text":"No challenge at all"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"With the average toji over 60 and the domestic industry shrinking, some breweries now employ women (excluded since Meiji) and foreigners out of necessity.","citation":"Lesson: The Toji Guild"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What does it mean that English borrowed words like umami and wabi-sabi?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"A linguistic gap reveals a cultural gap: English met a concept it had not previously named"},{"id":"q21b","text":"English has every word already"},{"id":"q21c","text":"The words mean nothing"},{"id":"q21d","text":"Borrowing words is a mistake"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"When a language must borrow a word, it is evidence of a concept the borrowing culture recognized but had no way to say, so the gap in words reveals a gap in culture.","citation":"Lesson: Words English Had to Borrow"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"What does the umami story show about language and perception?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"Western science could not recognize umami as a distinct taste for nearly a century because it had no word for it"},{"id":"q22b","text":"Umami does not exist"},{"id":"q22c","text":"Everyone always perceived umami"},{"id":"q22d","text":"Language has no effect on perception"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Ikeda named umami in 1908, but Western science did not accept it as a fifth taste until receptors were confirmed in 2002, showing the available vocabulary shapes what we can perceive.","citation":"Lesson: Words English Had to Borrow"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What does wabi-sabi mean?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"Beauty found specifically in imperfection and impermanence"},{"id":"q23b","text":"Perfect symmetry and permanence"},{"id":"q23c","text":"A type of rice"},{"id":"q23d","text":"A sake grade"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Wabi-sabi combines wabi (simplicity and humility) and sabi (the beauty of aging and impermanence) into beauty found in the imperfect and the transient.","citation":"Lesson: The Beauty of Passing"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"How does sake's freshness imperative contrast with whiskey?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"Premium sake is valued for being fresh and brief, the economic opposite of whiskey's aging premium"},{"id":"q24b","text":"Both are valued only when old"},{"id":"q24c","text":"Sake must age 20 years"},{"id":"q24d","text":"Neither changes with time"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"Premium sake is best fresh within months, so its brief peak is part of its value, the opposite of whiskey, where age adds value, two cultures answering value oppositely.","citation":"Lesson: The Beauty of Passing"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"What does mono no aware mean?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"A bittersweet awareness that all things pass, and that their transience is itself beautiful"},{"id":"q25b","text":"A guarantee that nothing changes"},{"id":"q25c","text":"A brewing technique"},{"id":"q25d","text":"A Korean rice wine"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"Mono no aware, the pathos of things, is the bittersweet sense that all things pass, symbolized by the cherry blossom that is beautiful precisely because it lasts only a week.","citation":"Lesson: The Beauty of Passing"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"Who was Otomo no Tabito?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"An eighth-century Japanese poet whose 13 tanka praising sake appear in the Man'yoshu"},{"id":"q26b","text":"A modern sake brewer"},{"id":"q26c","text":"A Korean king"},{"id":"q26d","text":"A Persian poet"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"Otomo no Tabito (665 to 731 CE) wrote a famous set of 13 tanka in praise of sake, collected in the Man'yoshu, Japan's oldest poetry anthology.","citation":"Lesson: The Beauty of Passing"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"How does sake poetry differ from Persian wine poetry?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"Sake poetry emphasizes authentic emotion and impermanence; Persian wine poetry emphasizes divine love and transcendence"},{"id":"q27b","text":"They are identical"},{"id":"q27c","text":"Neither uses metaphor"},{"id":"q27d","text":"Sake poetry rejects all feeling"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"Both use a drink to reach deeper truth, but through different frameworks: Persian poetry through divine love, Japanese sake poetry through authentic emotion and accepting impermanence.","citation":"Lesson: The Beauty of Passing"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"How is sake rice different from agave in what it needs?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"Sake rice thrives with care, rich water-retentive soil and ample water, the opposite of stress-loving agave"},{"id":"q28b","text":"It needs drought and poor soil"},{"id":"q28c","text":"It grows without water"},{"id":"q28d","text":"It is identical to agave"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Unlike stress-specialist agave, sake rice is an aquatic crop that wants rich, water-retentive soil and ample flooding to develop its starchy core.","citation":"Lesson: The Koji Path (intro)"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"What does junmai mean on a sake label?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"Pure rice sake, with no added alcohol"},{"id":"q29b","text":"The cheapest sake"},{"id":"q29c","text":"Sake aged 10 years"},{"id":"q29d","text":"A Korean rice wine"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"Junmai means pure rice, indicating sake made with no added distilled alcohol, one part of the seimaibuai-based grade ladder.","citation":"Lesson: The Polishing Ratio"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"What is a kura?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"A sake brewery"},{"id":"q30b","text":"A rice field"},{"id":"q30c","text":"A Shinto shrine"},{"id":"q30d","text":"A poetry anthology"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"A kura is a sake brewery, where precision and tradition meet, and small kura increasingly survive on premium and export sales.","citation":"Lesson: Volume to Value"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"What happened to women in Japanese sake brewing during the Meiji period?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"Industrialization increasingly excluded them, though they were the original keepers of fermentation"},{"id":"q31b","text":"They took over all breweries"},{"id":"q31c","text":"Nothing changed"},{"id":"q31d","text":"They invented koji"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"As sake production industrialized during the Meiji period (1868 to 1912), women were increasingly excluded, echoing how women were displaced from European brewing.","citation":"Lesson: Sake and the Sacred"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"Why did Japan's koji recognition matter beyond sake?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"It acknowledged a single microorganism as a foundational element of cultural identity and cuisine"},{"id":"q32b","text":"Koji is only used in sake"},{"id":"q32c","text":"It banned the mold"},{"id":"q32d","text":"It had no significance"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Naming koji the National Mold recognized that one organism underlies sake, miso, soy sauce, and mirin, making it foundational cultural infrastructure.","citation":"Lesson: Koji, the National Mold"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"How has sake export value changed from 2010 to 2023?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"It grew from about 60 million dollars to more than 290 million dollars"},{"id":"q33b","text":"It fell to zero"},{"id":"q33c","text":"It stayed at 60 million"},{"id":"q33d","text":"Exports are illegal"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"While domestic sake declined, exports grew from about 60 million dollars in 2010 to more than 290 million by 2023, led by the United States and China.","citation":"Lesson: Volume to Value"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"What does the Shinto kagami biraki ceremony involve?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"Ceremonially opening a sake barrel at celebrations, sharing sake with the kami"},{"id":"q34b","text":"Burning the rice harvest"},{"id":"q34c","text":"Polishing rice grains"},{"id":"q34d","text":"Closing a shrine"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"Kagami biraki is the ceremonial opening of a sake barrel at celebrations, marking communal transitions and sharing sake with the kami.","citation":"Lesson: Sake and the Sacred"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"What is a loanword, as illustrated by umami and terroir?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"A word borrowed from another language for a concept the borrowing language could not name"},{"id":"q35b","text":"A word that means nothing"},{"id":"q35c","text":"A type of poem"},{"id":"q35d","text":"A brewing tool"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"A loanword is borrowed whole from another language, like umami or terroir, because the receiving language had no equivalent for the concept.","citation":"Lesson: Words English Had to Borrow"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"What does makgeolli's history show about colonialism's cultural harm?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"Suppressing a traditional fermentation practice suppressed a form of community and cultural identity"},{"id":"q36b","text":"It improved Korean culture"},{"id":"q36c","text":"It had no effect"},{"id":"q36d","text":"It only changed prices"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"Because makgeolli was woven into farming life, household skill, and community, suppressing it during the occupation suppressed a living form of Korean cultural practice.","citation":"Lesson: The Suppression of Makgeolli"}]}
